shortTitle: Your Life As The Best Hitman

content.text: You are sitting in your kitchen awake at 3:11 a.m., the night drawing tight around the windows, the silence pressing down so hard your heartbeat is the loudest thing in the room. The city outside is asleep or pretending. You are not.
content.imageDescription: Wide shot of a dimly lit kitchen at night. The protagonist sits alone at the kitchen table, illuminated by faint streetlight through the window. The outside cityscape is dark, the window panes reflecting the person's tense gaze. Their hand rests on the table, veins visible from tension, as the night presses around them.

content.text: The phone on the counter vibrates once—a deep, bone-level rattle, not the friendly chirp of normal business, not the double buzz meant for emergencies. Just one clean pulse, as if the building itself is exhaling.
content.imageDescription: Close-up of a black phone vibrating on the kitchen counter, captured mid-buzz. The room is still, and the energy from the phone's vibration subtly ripples through a mug of untouched coffee beside it.

content.text: You look at the screen. One name. No photo, no contact details. Never needed them.
content.imageDescription: Over-the-shoulder view of the phone screen in the protagonist's hand, displaying just a single unsaved name in stark text, no photo or additional info. The background is blurred, heightening focus on the mysterious caller ID.

content.text: You answer without saying your name. You never say your name.
content.imageDescription: Cropped side view of the protagonist lifting the phone to their ear, remaining silent, their expression calm but watchful. The ambient light glints off the phone. The background is poorly lit, emphasizing isolation.

content.text: The voice on the other end is as familiar as your own, only quieter. He speaks only once: "It’s time." That’s all. No pleasantries, no explanations. There never are.
content.imageDescription: Close-up of the protagonist’s ear, with the phone pressed against it. An imagined echo or faint aura suggests a familiar male voice speaking from the other end, with the words “It’s time” visually implied in the air.

content.text: You set the phone face-down on the cold countertop. The silence grows heavier, dense as packed snow. You let yourself feel nothing—no anticipation, no dread, just the shallow breathing of a man whose routine is oxygen.
content.imageDescription: Top-down angle showing the protagonist’s hand placing the phone face down on the countertop. Their reflection barely visible in the glossy surface. The kitchen silence is palpable, with soft breath fogging in the cold air.

content.text: You move through your apartment without turning on a light. You have memorized every corner, every give in the floorboards, every shadow.
content.imageDescription: Wide shot from behind, as the protagonist walks through a darkened apartment, navigating confidently by memory. Furniture and objects cast long, indistinct shadows; the layout reveals routine and habit.

content.text: You open the drawer beside your bed, slicking your fingers along the grain of wood until you find the case within. Black, scuffed, latches worn smooth from being thumbed open and closed all these years.
content.imageDescription: Close-up of a hand gliding inside a bedside drawer, fingertips feeling the wood before gripping a weathered black case inside, its latches worn glossy from repeated use.

content.text: You unpack your tools, laying them out on the gray comforter in the same order as always: weapon, suppressor, extra magazine, polished blade, gloves, plastic sheeting folded dull-side out, burner phone, heavy envelope. Each piece gleams coldly in the streetlight filtering through the blinds.
content.imageDescription: Overhead shot of a gray bedspread with a meticulous spread of tools: a pistol, suppressor, spare magazine, shiny knife, disposable gloves, folded plastic, a burner phone, and a heavy envelope. Streetlight slants through blinds, creating sharp patterns.

content.text: You inspect the firearm, thumb running along the ridged slide, thick with the smell of gun oil, metallic and sharp. The edges of the magazine bite into your palm, grounding you in the simplicity of purpose. Everything in its place. Every motion deliberate. Nothing wasted.
content.imageDescription: Close-up of hands expertly checking and assembling a firearm and magazine, each movement methodical. The metallic sheen and slight reflectivity catch the ambient city light, detailed veins and scars visible on the hands.

content.text: You clean your hands twice. Under your nails, up your wrists, around the knuckles where skin splits open in winter. Surgical precision. You pull on the gloves, latex first, then black leather. The snap as they settle over your fingers is a whisper. You breathe out.
content.imageDescription: Medium shot of the protagonist at the sink, washing hands with almost medical detail. Then, a close-up as they layer latex gloves, followed by black leather gloves, the tight fit making their knuckles faintly bulge.

content.text: You check the time: 3:17. You have two hours. The message was clear in its brevity. If there is a clock in this work, you are always its second hand, never the hour.
content.imageDescription: Clocks on the stove and wall both read 3:17 a.m. A wristwatch is adjusted; the protagonist’s gloved finger traces the ticking second hand, alluding to the unyielding passage of time.

content.text: You pull the blinds up two inches, scan the street below. The silver car is where it should be, unlocked, engine off, interior as clean as a doctor's office. No one in sight. This is normal. Normal is a word you can still pretend to believe.
content.imageDescription: Low angle from inside, looking through a small gap in the blinds. The protagonist’s eyes are just visible. Down on the quiet street, a lone silver car is parked under a streetlamp, showing no signs of disturbance.

content.text: You move through your preparations as if guided by a script you’ve memorized, iterations refined by time and pressure. You dress in dark clothing, simple, anonymous, not black but a color somewhere between iron and stone. Your shoes are unremarkable, scuffed at the toe, soles sticky with the city’s secrets.
content.imageDescription: Montage style shot: clothes laid out and being donned—dark, blending with shadows—followed by an image of boots on a worn mat, scuffed toes and city residue detailed.

content.text: The jacket is heavier tonight. You double-check your pockets: knife, ampoule, tape, a folded photo. The face, blurred by the motion of living, stares back. You study it only for as long as necessary. Just enough. Not more.
content.imageDescription: Close-up of the protagonist checking his jacket pockets. A folded, blurry photo is briefly held at eye level, the target’s face indistinct but troubling, then swiftly returned to the pocket.

content.text: The building you’re going to has three entrances. You know the alleyway stretches behind, lit only by the green glow of an ancient exit sign and the lazy loop of a security camera that doesn’t record. You know who is inside. You know where they sit, how their shadow falls on the carpet. You have walked this approach a thousand times in memory, each step as methodical as the steps before it. There is no such thing as improvisation. Even chance is expected.
content.imageDescription: Exterior wide shot of a nondescript apartment building with three separate entrances and a narrow, dark alley behind, dimly illuminated by a flickering green exit sign. A security camera points lazily, wires dangling.

content.text: You pick up your bag—unmarked, heavy, indistinct—and leave by the back door. You lock the door behind you. Always. You listen to the soft grind of the deadbolt, the way the metal catches, the small assurance of routine.
content.imageDescription: Medium shot of the protagonist, bag over shoulder, slipping out a rear exit. Their hand lingers on the lock, turning it with practiced care, while a red EXIT sign glows dimly above.

content.text: As you walk to the car, the cool air sharpens each breath. You taste the city: wet concrete, distant oil, old cigarettes. Every sense dialed in, you can count your footsteps by the way your heel lands on worn sidewalk seams. Eighty-seven steps to the curb. Fourteen to the car.
content.imageDescription: Side shot along an empty city sidewalk at night as the protagonist walks briskly, counting steps, their breath visible in the cold air. The sidewalk’s worn lines and city grime are apparent under their feet.

content.text: You slide into the driver’s seat and start the engine by touch, letting your mind settle into the hum of a machine built for moving things farther away.
content.imageDescription: Interior shot from the passenger side, as the protagonist smooths into the car’s front seat and calmly starts the silent engine, dashboard lights blooming softly.

content.text: You take the back roads, keeping to the shadows cast by sleeping streetlights and stubborn flyers from last year’s election. No music, no radio. Only the low static hush that fills the space when the city is holding its breath.
content.imageDescription: Wide city street at night, empty but for the lone car gliding past pools of light under old lampposts. Torn election flyers are posted on utility poles, fluttering in the breeze.

content.text: You check your mirrors—once, twice, randomly a third time. No tail. No interruption. In this business, being sure is a kind of religion.
content.imageDescription: Rear-view mirror reflection of intense eyes darting between the center and side mirrors, scanning for pursuers. The empty street is visible in the night rain’s faint glow.

content.text: You park three blocks away. Never directly in front. Never in sight of any likely exit. You lock the car, pocket the keys in your jacket, and slip down the alley beside the bakery. The bricks are slick, cool under your hand—smell of swelling yeast and yesterday’s coffee.
content.imageDescription: Dark alley scene: The protagonist moves quietly between shadowed brick walls, one hand steadying against the slick surface, glancing behind at a dimly lit bakery’s back entrance.

content.text: You listen for the rasp of your own breathing inside your mask. Every sound registers: a distant cat, rain pinging metal, tires hissing three streets away. You wait in the mouth of the alley, heartbeat steady, counting off each second.
content.imageDescription: Tight shot of the protagonist partially masked, still against the alley wall, eyes closed and listening keenly to faint city noise: cat cries, raindrops, distant tires. Their breath fogs slightly in the cold.

content.text: You test the first door. Always locked. You test the second—a metal side door with old scratches by the hinge, the sort you know hides replaced locks but sloppy habit. You slip a thin tool from your pocket and in twelve seconds you are inside, moving up narrow stairs, soft footfalls on the carpet faded by years of secrets.
content.imageDescription: Close-up of a gloved hand testing two doors, focusing especially on a battered metal door with visible scratches. The lock is expertly picked and the door nudged open, revealing a shadowy, narrow staircase inside.

content.text: You smell mildew and something like tears, but mostly you smell your own certainty. The inside air tastes ancient.
content.imageDescription: Medium hallway shot, protagonist halfway up a creaking, carpeted stairwell. The peeling wallpaper and dim hallway light heighten the old, musty atmosphere.

content.text: At the top, you wait. Three doors. The one you want is at the end, numbers peeled, brass slot catching the hall’s yellow light. You thumb the blade, not for use but for belief. You always have options, even if you never use them.
content.imageDescription: Perspective from the stairs, looking down a cramped hallway lined with three doors. The protagonist stands before the battered door at the end, hand on a sheathed blade, thumb hovering over its grip.

content.text: You take the photo out one last time. Remind yourself: just a job. Just another name on a list. You listen. Television, voices, the muffled scrape of chair legs. You stand there long enough to name each sound, to map the square footage of every word spoken inside.
content.imageDescription: Close-up of the blurred photo in the protagonist's hand again. The protagonist's ear tilts toward the door, picking up faint sounds from within—a voice on TV, low talk, scraping chairs—all registering in their gaze.

content.text: You knock once—soft, but hard enough to matter. Not the kind of knock that asks for anything.
content.imageDescription: Focused shot of a gloved hand knocking purposefully on the weathered door, the hallway’s shadows stretching behind.

content.text: Footsteps near the threshold, the deadbolt turns, and the door opens ten centimeters. A man peers out, eyes wide with the unmistakable understanding that his life just changed. You show him nothing—a memory in the shape of a silhouette. He opens the door all the way. Out of habit or resignation, it doesn’t matter.
content.imageDescription: Medium shot from inside the apartment: door ajar, the target's anxious face peering out. The protagonist stands as a dark silhouette, features unreadable, as the door swings slowly open further.

content.text: Inside, the air is close. Heavy with dust, the iron tang of old radiator heat, the ghost of last night’s whiskey. You step forward, keeping to the edges, back to the wall, eyes tracking every corner. There is a woman in the back hallway, another man on the couch. You see their faces. You remember them. You will have to.
content.imageDescription: Wide interior shot of the dim apartment, filled with shadows and faded furniture. The protagonist slides along the wall, three people distributed: a nervous woman in the hallway, a tense man slouched on the couch, all their faces visible and wary.

content.text: You speak exactly three words—calm, professional, instructions as quiet as snow: "You. Sit. Quietly." No one argues. There is something about your manner, about the calm in your voice, that tells them this is not a night for hesitation or pleading.
content.imageDescription: The protagonist, standing authoritatively, gestures with a gloved hand as the three occupants settle into different chairs, their faces pale and hands visible, silent compliance on display.

content.text: You direct them each to separate chairs, hands where you can see them, phones placed on the table one at a time. You watch their faces as you move; the flicker behind the eyes, the trembling hands they try to keep still. You make note of everything. You always do.
content.imageDescription: Medium shot as each person in turn places a phone on the table. The protagonist's eyes follow their every movement, cataloguing their subtle nervous tics and tells.

content.text: You confirm the target. No surprises. The information is good—never a guarantee but tonight, it’s all you have. The man doesn’t say your name. That is correct. He looks at you, sees who you are, what you are, and he asks, quietly, "How did you—" but you cut him off with a look. It isn’t his job to ask questions. It isn’t yours to answer them. Not anymore.
content.imageDescription: Close-up of the target’s anxious face searching the protagonist's for answers. The protagonist’s chilling, silent gaze cuts off any attempt at conversation, the room’s tension palpable.

content.text: In the moments before, you hear the refrigerator kick on, the heavy tick of the old wall clock as it marks what little is left. You draw the weapon, attach the suppressor—an action so practiced it feels like lighting a match.
content.imageDescription: Tight close-up on the protagonist screwing a suppressor onto the barrel of the gun. Background includes the blurred motion of a wall clock’s second hand and the hum of the refrigerator.

content.text: The target’s breathing goes shallow. Out of the corner of your vision, you see the woman’s foot twitch, her fingers curling around the armrest so tightly her knuckles go white. You taste the air, metallic, every breath a countdown. You do not feel adrenaline, only clarity. Only precision. You have done this forty-seven times, in different rooms, different countries, under different names. Always with the same result.
content.imageDescription: Slow-motion effect as the woman’s foot trembles, her hand white-knuckled on the chair. The target’s chest rises and falls shallowly. The protagonist’s measured, steady breathing mists slightly in the still room.

content.text: You steady your hand. One shot. Not rushed. Not hesitated. The sound is nothing. A snap swallowed by the carpet and the closed window. The target falls sideways, slack.
content.imageDescription: Freeze-frame at the instant the gun is fired: a small puff from the suppressor, and the target collapses sideways out of his chair in a blur. The rest of the room is utterly still.

content.text: The remainder frozen, eyes wide, mouths open and silent. You keep your focus. You lower the weapon, scan the room, scan again. You wipe the grip, the trigger, the latch. No prints. No residue. You pack away the weapon, slide on fresh gloves, and pull the bag from your shoulder.
content.imageDescription: View through the protagonist’s eyes: the two witnesses are in shock, frozen. The protagonist methodically cleans the gun, swaps gloves, and pulls a black bag from his shoulder.

content.text: You turn to the others. The script says witnesses are a loose thread. Tonight, your orders are explicit. Only one. Only the contract. The rest are warnings. You meet their eyes, one at a time, and tell them to remember nothing. To forget your voice, your face, everything but the fear that will live under their skin for the rest of their life. Sometimes this is enough. Sometimes it is not.
content.imageDescription: The protagonist stands before the surviving witnesses, meeting each person's fearful gaze one by one. Their faces register horror and confusion as he delivers the chilling, silent warning. Shadows loom heavily across the walls.

content.text: You move to the door, open it quietly, listen for footsteps on the stairs. Silence. You slip out, another shadow in a building full of them.
content.imageDescription: The protagonist gently pushes the door open, pausing in the threshold. No footsteps echo; only dim light seeps in as he melts back into the empty hallway, unseen.

content.text: Down the stairwell, every creak registers in your bones. You step through the exit into a rain that has just begun. The sky is bruise-dark, the world glassy with water and regret.
content.imageDescription: Wide shot of a stairwell descending to a side exit. The protagonist pushes through to a city street slick with fresh rain. Puddles reflect streetlights; the sky is dark and heavy.

content.text: You cross the street, keeping to the edges, moving with the unremarkable pace of a man for whom time is elastic. You reach the car without incident.
content.imageDescription: Profile shot of the protagonist walking across the wet street, hugging the shadows, face obscured under a hood. The parked car waits in darkness across the way.

content.text: You sit for a moment, gloved hands on the wheel, breath fogging the windshield. You watch for headlights, pedestrians, any irregularity in the periphery. None come.
content.imageDescription: Interior shot from the dashboard: the protagonist sits, hands poised but unmoving on the steering wheel. His breath clouds the windshield faintly as his eyes dart over the rearview mirror, scanning all around.

content.text: You drive. Three turns, a loop through a tunnel, and you are gone from the scene, as if you were never there.
content.imageDescription: City street tunnel view, car speeding through shadow and light. The protagonist behind the wheel, tension fading, becomes just another anonymous driver vanishing into the night.

content.text: This is the part you like least. The drive home. The city’s glow feels hostile. Every stoplight, every unfamiliar vehicle in your mirror, is a question you answer with composure. You watch as dawn begins to bleed over rooftops, hesitant, cold.
content.imageDescription: Night-to-dawn transition as the car passes glowing city lights. The protagonist’s eyes in the mirror, unsettled. The sky turns from pitch black to a frosty gray-blue, light cresting distant rooftops.

content.text: The phone chirps once. The same voice, the same brevity: "Done?" You type a single word reply. You never use names, never locations. Just confirmation. The language of men who don’t exist.
content.imageDescription: Inside the parked car, the protagonist’s phone lights up with a terse message. He replies with a single word—seen reflected in his dark eyes—then deletes the conversation instantly.

content.text: You park five blocks from your apartment, burn the gloves with chemical wipes, toss the bag in a trash bin that will be emptied by morning. Another layer gone.
content.imageDescription: Alleyway view as the protagonist, hands gloved, cleans and burns his gloves with wipes and throws the tool bag into a grungy trash bin beside graffiti.

content.text: You walk home, footsteps echoing, shoes leaving nothing but a faint notch in the night’s memory.
content.imageDescription: Wide city sidewalk at dawn. The protagonist walks alone, his silhouette stretching ahead, footsteps imprinted briefly on wet pavement before vanishing.

content.text: You enter your apartment, shed your jacket, wash your hands for a third time. The smell of latex and sweat and something older lingers.
content.imageDescription: Overhead shot in a small bathroom, jacket draped over a chair as the protagonist scrubs his hands, hair damp with sweat, mirror fogged with breath and low light.

content.text: You sit at the kitchen table. The clock on the wall is wrong, slow by a minute or two, but you forgive it. Forgiveness comes easy to objects, never to people.
content.imageDescription: Kitchen table close-up: the protagonist, slouched wearily, gazes at a slightly-wrong wall clock. A sense of exhaustion and resigned acceptance in their eyes.

content.text: Twenty minutes later, the phone rings again. This time, the number is blocked. This does not happen. Not for you. You answer anyway.
content.imageDescription: Dark close-up of the phone ringing with ‘Blocked Number’ displayed. The protagonist’s hand hesitates slightly before picking up on the first ring.

content.text: The voice isn’t the one you expect. It’s older, rougher, the kind of voice that has never smiled. It says, "We have a meeting. Tomorrow. Noon. Bring nothing." The line goes dead.
content.imageDescription: Close side profile of the protagonist’s tense face as a cold, unfamiliar voice issues instructions through the phone. The sudden click of the call ending leaves the air heavy.

content.text: You sit there, elbows on the table, hands clean but shaking in ways you try not to notice. You’ve operated under a simple principle all your life—distance is safety. Precision is freedom. But distance just closed. You’ve never met the person behind the phone. You were never meant to. Rules, you always believed those mattered. Now, rules feel like stories you told yourself to make the fear feel organized.
content.imageDescription: Medium shot of the protagonist still at the kitchen table, elbows resting, hands trembling slightly above the surface. His gaze is fixed on nothing, haunted by the unraveling of his old certainties.

content.text: You look up at the ceiling. A crack runs through the paint, reminding you of all the old, small fissures that never closed. You think about the jobs before this one. Forty-seven rooms, forty-seven stories erased. You remember every moment. You remember none of them at all.
content.imageDescription: Upward angle as the protagonist tilts back to stare at a spreading crack across the ceiling. Ghostly overlays of faces and blurred rooms suggest flashbacks to past jobs, indistinct and haunting.

content.text: You try to sleep. You don’t. When the sun burns through the curtain in the gray morning, you brew weak coffee, stare at the city. A bus rumbles by, anonymous people in anonymous shoes, carrying secrets you’ll never know. You see yourself in the window, only a shadow behind the glass. You practice looking like someone else.
content.imageDescription: Daybreak in the kitchen: a weak stream of sunlight cuts through curtains. The protagonist stands at the window with a mug, looking at their own faint reflection. Outside, commuters pass, faces blurred in anonymity.

content.text: You call your sister. She answers. Her voice is curious, tired, untouched by whatever passes for truth in your life. She wants to know if you’re coming for the holidays. You say maybe. You say you hope so. She tells you she loves you. You burn the taste of her voice into your memory, another shield you can carry into whatever comes next. You hang up. You sit with the space her words leave behind.
content.imageDescription: The protagonist on the phone, a rare softness in their eyes. Over-the-shoulder shot reveals a photo of a smiling woman (the sister) pinned on the fridge. As the call ends, the protagonist’s face is caught between longing and sadness.

content.text: You pack a folder. Inside—no names, no signatures, only dates and faces and addresses written in your hand. Your handwriting is careful, methodical, shaped by years of needing to say things that will never be said aloud. You seal the folder, address it not to police, not to lawyers, but to someone who might understand what needs to be done. A journalist, maybe. Someone who has spent years dusting for the fingerprints of men like you. Each fact, each admission, each buried truth arranged with the same discipline you bring to every contract: nothing omitted, nothing added, no room for error.
content.imageDescription: Tight focus on hands assembling a thick dossier: photos, scribbled notes, addresses—all evidence, but without direct names. The protagonist addresses an envelope marked only with a nondescript recipient. The mood is tense, final.

content.text: You walk to a mailbox three neighborhoods away, drop the folder inside. You watch the lid close. The sound, metallic and final, is louder than any gunshot. You feel the moment settle into your bones.
content.imageDescription: The protagonist, jacket collar up, walks down a distant street. He stands before an old blue public mailbox and drops the fat envelope in. The mailbox lid clangs shut, echoing in the morning stillness.

content.text: You return home. Shower, shave, change into new clothes. You check the time. The meeting is in one hour. You sit by the window, watching the city wake up again, slow and indifferent. You feel the ripple of an old fear—one reserved for soldiers on the last day of war, for men who know how their story will end.
content.imageDescription: Montage: protagonist showering, shaving, clean shirt pulled on. He sits by the window in new clothes, staring at brightening city streets with pensive dread. A subtle shiver betrays his unease.

content.text: You leave your apartment with nothing in your hands. You walk with the practiced anonymity of a man who has spent his life being invisible. Every step is measured, every glance weighted. You do not look back. You do not look forward. You walk into the day, into the meeting, into whatever price must finally be paid for work done so perfectly it erased itself.
content.imageDescription: Wide exterior shot from behind as the protagonist leaves his building empty-handed. He blends in with commuters, but his posture is tightly controlled, his face unreadable. He disappears into the city's crowds.

content.text: There are no more rules. Only the shape of consequences, winding out beyond you, over every name and silence you carried. As the building door closes, you let the last memory—the smell of latex, the gun’s cold weight, your sister’s voice—fade to nothing. After all these years, this story, your story, may finally have someone to listen.
content.imageDescription: Interior of a lobby as large doors close behind the protagonist’s silhouette, shutting him off from the street. His figure is reflected faintly on polished stone, and symbolic items—the gloves, the gun, his sister’s voice—hint as ephemeral echoes fading around him.
